name: manual-live-file-surgery-can-leave-stale-connection-state
tags: stale connection, readonly database, manual db surgery, checkpoint, restart verification, sqlite
body:
After manually stopping a server, swapping/replacing a file it has open (e.g. `mv`/`os.replace` on a SQLite DB), and restarting — don't assume the very next restart is fully clean, even once the equivalent logic is later wired into proper startup code. During today's checkpoint-DB compaction work (task:5afd1b61/b623ea48), a manual live swap (stop server → checkpoint WAL → backup → compact → swap → restart) was later followed by code-driven restarts via `_compact_checkpoint_db()` in `lifespan()` — and one of those subsequent restarts still produced `attempt to write a readonly database` from `get_current_session()`, resolved only by one more explicit clean restart (`launchctl kickstart -k`) and a live verification call.

**Why:** The exact mechanism wasn't fully diagnosed (plausible theory: a persistent connection or cached file handle from the manual-surgery era lingered across an otherwise-successful restart), but the fix was simple and the failure was caught immediately — specifically because task:117e44a4's earlier timing/exception logging was already in place. Without it, this would have silently degraded again with zero signal, the same failure mode as the original 10s+ hang.

**How to apply:** After any manual intervention on a file backing a live process's persistent connection, verify with an actual functional call after the next restart (not just a health-check ping) before considering the intervention complete — especially when overlapping manual and code-driven fixes happen in the same session.
